summary: The chapter's conceptual core is the KGW green-list watermark: a secret-key-seeded green/red partition of the vocabulary, a logit boost that biases sampling toward green tokens, and a z-score detector that separates human text (Binomial(T,gamma) null) from watermarked text. Around this sit the motivating asymmetry argument (verification vs classification), robustness decay under attack, and the multimodal/C2PA/post-hoc branches. The two existing figures are both plumbing diagrams (serving-loop processor placement; image encode/decode pipeline) and render well in both themes, but NEITHER visualizes the actual statistical mechanism or the null-vs-watermarked separation that the entire chapter turns on. That is the foundational gap. I keep both existing figures and add the green-list mechanism (generation+detection twin, sharing the same key-seeded hash), the detection-distribution separation with threshold and robustness decay, and the motivating asymmetry-inversion contrast.
core_intuitions: Watermarking inverts the attacker/defender asymmetry: post-hoc detection is an open-ended CLASSIFICATION problem (defender must model every model/attack), while watermark detection is a bounded VERIFICATION problem whose hardness scales with a secret key.

The KGW mechanism: a hash of (secret key + previous tokens) deterministically partitions the vocabulary into a green list and a red list at every step; generation boosts green logits by delta, so sampled text carries an excess of green tokens.

Detection recomputes the SAME partition from the same key+context and counts green tokens g; under the human null g ~ Binomial(T, gamma), so z=(g - gamma*T)/sqrt(T*gamma*(1-gamma)) turns 'looks AI' into a calibrated statistical test (z>~4 => FPR ~1e-5).

Robustness is distribution-sliding: an edit/paraphrase attack randomizes a fraction of tokens, sliding the watermarked green-count distribution back toward the null; the watermark survives until that fraction is large enough to push z below the threshold.

Watermarking, C2PA provenance, and post-hoc detection are complementary because they cover different attack surfaces (watermark survives metadata stripping; C2PA survives lossless format changes and carries who/when/how; post-hoc handles unknown origin but is Goodhart-fragile).
Image/audio watermarks live in continuous signal space via a learned encoder/decoder and must survive lossy channels (JPEG, resize, crop), unlike the discrete-token text scheme.

existing.name: wmprov-vllm-logits-processor
existing.issue: Clean and correct in both themes; clearly shows WHERE the WatermarkLogitsProcessor sits in the autoregressive decode loop (logit vector -> hash/build green_set/add delta -> sampling -> KV update -> feedback of prev_tokens). It conveys the serving PLUMBING well but deliberately does not show the mechanism math or detection; that is fine and is covered by the new mechanism figure.
existing.improvement: No change needed. It correctly stays at the systems-integration altitude.

existing.name: wmprov-image-wm-pipeline
existing.issue: Renders correctly in both themes; the two-channel layout (ideal channel vs lossy channel with JPEG/resize/crop before the decoder) is exactly the right intuition for why image watermarks need learned robustness. Minor: the dark subtitle text on the purple encoder/decoder blocks and the second-line labels sit close to the box edges, but they remain legible in both themes.
existing.improvement: Optional polish only: add a touch of vertical padding inside the purple NN blocks so the second-line captions ('adds steganographic signal', 'projects onto detection vector') clear the box edge with margin; keep the two-channel structure and labels unchanged.

new_figures.name: wmprov-greenlist-mechanism
new_figures.concept: The KGW green-list watermark as a symmetric generation/detection pair driven by one shared key-seeded hash.
new_figures.rationale: This is the beating heart of the chapter and is currently unserved: the two existing figures show serving plumbing and an image pipeline, but nothing shows the actual partition-boost-count mechanism the whole text (the code, the z-score, the robustness table, the EU-Act discussion) rests on. Seeing that BOTH sides recompute the identical green/red split from the same secret key is the single 'aha' that makes watermarking-as-verification concrete.
new_figures.brief: A twin-panel infographic joined by a shared central element. CENTER (spanning): a rounded 'seed' box labeled 'Hash( secret key k , previous h tokens )'. An arrow fans out from it to a horizontal strip representing a TOY vocabulary of about 10 token cells (small illustrative integers only, e.g. tokens t0..t9); roughly half the cells are tinted with a positive/accent 'green-list' role and labeled 'green list (gamma|V|)', the rest with a muted/neutral 'red-list' role labeled 'red list'. Make explicit with a small note that this partition is deterministic per step and identical on both sides. LEFT PANEL 'Generation (operator holds key)': show a small bar chart of base logits over those ~10 tokens; the green-list bars get a visibly added segment labeled '+delta'; an arrow 'softmax + sample' leads to a highlighted picked cell with caption 'sampled token — biased toward green'. Add a one-line note 'no weight changes; only logits nudged'. RIGHT PANEL 'Detection (verifier re-derives same green list)': show a short candidate token sequence; each token gets a small check/cross for green/red membership under the recomputed partition; a tally 'green tokens g of T'; then the boxed formula z = (g - gamma*T) / sqrt(T*gamma*(1-gamma)) with a caption 'excess green => large z => AI-generated'. Emphasize the mirror symmetry: the same center hash box feeds arrows into BOTH panels (draw the two arrows leaving the shared partition strip). Keep gamma shown as one-half and delta as a small positive value; invent NO benchmark numbers. Theme-safe: use semantic accent roles for green/red and boost, neutral strokes for boxes; everything inside the viewBox with clear left-to-right, top-to-bottom reading order.

new_figures.name: wmprov-detection-separation
new_figures.concept: Human vs watermarked green-token count as two distributions, with the z* threshold and an attack that slides the watermarked distribution back toward the null.
new_figures.rationale: This makes two foundational ideas click at once: (1) why a z>~4 threshold yields a ~1e-5 false-positive rate — it is the tail of the human Binomial(T,gamma) null past the threshold; and (2) robustness as distribution-sliding — the worked example (clean z about 8, 40% paraphrase z about 4.7, heavy attack z below 4) becomes a single moving picture. Neither existing figure touches the statistical separation, which is the payoff of the whole scheme.
new_figures.brief: X-axis is 'number of green tokens g in a T-token passage'; y-axis is relative frequency. Draw a NULL/human bell centered at mu = gamma*T (label 'human text: Binomial(T, gamma), centered at gamma*T'). Draw a WATERMARKED bell shifted to the right (more green), clearly separated, labeled 'watermarked (clean)'. Place a vertical THRESHOLD line at 'z* (about 4 sigma above mu)'; shade the region to its right as 'flagged as AI'. Shade the tiny sliver of the human curve past the threshold and label it 'false positives ~ 1e-5' (order-of-magnitude tail, conceptual — not a measured benchmark). ANIMATION: a control labeled 'fraction of tokens paraphrased / edited' sweeps from 0 upward; as it rises, the watermarked bell slides LEFTWARD toward the null and its peak drops in z-distance; annotate the current z shrinking (e.g. a moving readout 'z' with small illustrative values consistent with the chapter: high when clean, near the threshold at moderate edit, below threshold at heavy edit); when the watermarked mass crosses to the left of the threshold, flip a caption to 'evades detection — but text now heavily rewritten'. STATIC DEFAULT FRAME (must be fully legible frozen): show THREE curves simultaneously — the human null at gamma*T, a clean watermark far right, and a 'moderate attack' watermark near the threshold — plus the z* line and the shaded flag region and human tail. Small illustrative integers only; do not invent detector accuracy numbers. Theme-safe: distribution fills use accent/positive roles, the null uses a neutral role, threshold is a strong neutral line; keep all curves and labels inside the viewBox with no overlap.

new_figures.name: wmprov-asymmetry-inversion
new_figures.concept: Why watermarking exists: post-hoc detection is open-ended classification; watermark detection is bounded key-based verification.
new_figures.rationale: The chapter opens on this asymmetry argument and returns to it (Goodhart, EU Act, open-weight gap) — it is the strategic 'why' that frames every mechanism that follows, yet it is purely verbal. A crisp contrast infographic turns the abstract 'inverts the asymmetry' sentence into an image the reader can hold onto for the rest of the chapter.
new_figures.brief: Two side-by-side panels under a shared title 'The same question, two problem types'. LEFT panel 'Post-hoc detection = CLASSIFICATION': depict a defender's decision boundary (a wavy, uncertain line) trying to separate a cloud of 'human text' points from a cloud of 'AI text' points, with the clouds overlapping in the middle (overlap zone labeled 'errors: false positives + missed'); around the AI cloud draw small tags for the moving targets the defender must anticipate — 'new model', 'paraphrase', 'translation roundtrip', 'unusual prompt'. Caption: 'defender must model every model and every attack -> arms race, high and unstable FPR'. RIGHT panel 'Watermarking = VERIFICATION': show generation emitting content stamped with a small key/lock motif ('signal embedded under secret key k'), and a detector performing a clean key-check that outputs a sharp binary 'match / no-match' with a calibrated p-value. Caption: 'hardness scales with the secret key, not with attacker sophistication -> a bounded, calibrated test'. BOTTOM strip spanning both: a bold arrow labeled 'watermarking inverts the asymmetry: open-ended classification -> bounded cryptographic verification'. Static, conceptual, NO numbers beyond a qualitative FPR contrast. Theme-safe: use a neutral role for the classification panel's uncertainty, an accent/positive role for the verification key motif; keep both clouds, tags, and captions inside the viewBox with clear separation.
